content.text: Basic Support

Azure Basic Support is the default support plan included at no cost with every Azure subscription. It provides access to self-help documentation, community forums, and billing support — but no technical support cases. Understanding its scope helps you decide whether to upgrade and when.

attrs.alt: Azure support plan comparison — Basic through Premier

content.text: What Basic Support Includes
Basic Support covers a specific set of channels that do not require a paid support plan. These are available 24 hours a day to all Azure subscribers regardless of subscription type.
Billing and Subscription Support

Billing support is the one area where Basic plan customers can open support cases. This includes disputes over charges, questions about invoice line items, subscription state issues (suspended, cancelled), and quota increase requests for common services.

# Open a billing support request via Azure CLI
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name "billing-inquiry-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
  --title "Invoice discrepancy for subscription 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000" \
  --description "Charged for VM hours not matching deployment logs for July 2026" \
  --problem-classification-id "/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/517f2da6-78fd-0498-4e22-ad26996b1dfc/problemClassifications/3ec1a070-f242-9ecf-5a7c-e1a88ce029ef" \
  --severity "minimal" \
  --contact-first-name "Dev" \
  --contact-last-name "Team" \
  --contact-email "devops@company.com" \
  --contact-country "US" \
  --contact-language "en-US" \
  --contact-timezone "Pacific Standard Time"
# The problem-classification-id for billing is a fixed GUID per service
Azure Service Health

Basic Support subscribers have full access to Azure Service Health, which provides personalised alerts for outages, planned maintenance, and health advisories affecting your specific subscriptions and regions.

# List active service issues affecting your subscription
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/events?api-version=2022-10-01" \
  --query "value[?properties.eventType=='ServiceIssue'].{title:properties.title, status:properties.status, startTime:properties.impactStartTime}" \
  --output table
Azure Advisor

Azure Advisor is a free personalized recommendation engine available to all subscribers. It analyses your deployed resources and produces actionable guidance across five pillars: cost, security, reliability, operational excellence, and performance.

# List high-impact Advisor recommendations
az advisor recommendation list \
  --filter "category eq 'Cost'" \
  --query "[?impact=='High'].{name:shortDescription.problem, impact:impact, resource:resourceMetadata.resourceId}" \
  --output table

# Generate Advisor score (overall posture indicator)
az advisor score list --output table
Documentation, Quickstarts, and Learn

Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com) and the Azure documentation portal are freely accessible without a support plan. They include interactive sandbox labs, certification learning paths, architecture reference guides, and API reference documentation.
What Basic Support Does NOT Include
The central limitation of Basic Support is the absence of any technical support channel. This affects several critical scenarios that teams frequently underestimate.
Scenario
Basic Plan
Requires Paid Plan
Production outage caused by Azure infrastructure
No technical support case — rely on Service Health only
Standard or higher (Sev A < 1 hr)
API or SDK behavior question
Community forums (Stack Overflow, Microsoft Q&A) only
Developer ($29/mo, business hours)
Architecture review before deployment
Documentation only
Professional Direct (advisory services)
Performance degradation diagnosis
Self-service diagnostics in Azure portal
Standard (Sev B < 4 hr)
Third-party software on Azure VMs
No support
Standard (best-effort)
Preview / beta service issues
No support
Developer or higher
Self-Service Channels Available on Basic
Microsoft Q&A

Microsoft Q&A (aka.ms/qna) is the official technical community forum where Microsoft engineers and MVPs respond to questions. It is indexed so Azure support engineers can reference it when escalations occur under paid plans.
Azure Diagnostics

The Azure portal embeds resource-specific diagnostics under the 'Diagnose and solve problems' blade. These run automated checks against your resource configuration and emit structured findings — useful for common issues like connectivity failures, App Service startup errors, and VM boot problems.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def run_resource_diagnostics(resource_id: str, detector_name: str) -> dict:
    """
    Invoke a portal diagnostic detector programmatically.
    detector_name examples: 'webapprestart', 'cpuanalysis', 'workerprocess'
    Available detectors vary by resource type.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{resource_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Portal/diagnostics/{detector_name}"
        f"?api-version=2019-01-01"
    )
    resp = requests.post(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=60)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

# Example: run web app restart diagnostic
APP_ID = "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/my-api"
result = run_resource_diagnostics(APP_ID, "webapprestart")
for insight in result.get("properties", {}).get("dataset", []):
    print(insight.get("table", {}).get("tableName"), "—", insight.get("renderingProperties", {}).get("title", ""))
Azure Monitor Metrics and Logs

Azure Monitor is free for standard platform metrics (retained 93 days) and basic activity logs (retained 90 days). Log Analytics workspaces for custom queries require a paid workspace but are not gated on the support plan tier.

# Query resource metrics without any support plan via CLI
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/my-api" \
  --metric "CpuTime" "Http5xx" "Requests" \
  --interval PT1H \
  --start-time 2026-06-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2026-07-01T00:00:00Z \
  --aggregation Total \
  --output table
Checking and Updating Your Support Plan
Every Azure subscription has exactly one support plan active at a time. The plan can be viewed and changed in the Azure portal under Help + Support > Support plans, or via the Azure Support REST API.
Querying the Active Support Plan via REST

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_support_plan(subscription_id: str) -> dict:
    """Return the active support plan for a subscription."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportPlanTypes"
        f"?api-version=2021-06-01-preview"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

result = get_support_plan("your-subscription-id")
for plan in result.get("value", []):
    print(plan["name"], "—", plan["properties"].get("planType", "unknown"))
Upgrading from Basic

Support plans are subscription-scoped — each subscription needs its own plan. Organisations with many subscriptions typically manage this via Azure Policy or a subscription vending pipeline that assigns the correct plan at provisioning time.

def check_subscriptions_without_paid_support(subscription_ids: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Return subscription IDs still on the Basic (free) plan."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    basic_subs = []

    for sub_id in subscription_ids:
        result = get_support_plan(sub_id)
        plans = result.get("value", [])
        paid = [p for p in plans if p["name"].lower() not in ("basic", "free")]
        if not paid:
            basic_subs.append(sub_id)

    return basic_subs

# Use the management group subscriptions list as input
import requests

def list_subscriptions_under_management_group(mg_id: str, token: str) -> list[str]:
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management"
        f"/managementGroups/{mg_id}/subscriptions"
        f"?api-version=2021-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return [s["name"] for s in resp.json().get("value", [])]
Azure Policy: Enforcing Support Plan Compliance

You can write a custom Azure Policy definition that flags subscriptions still on the Basic plan and surfaces them in Compliance dashboards. This is useful in enterprise environments where production subscriptions must have at least Standard support.
Note: The Microsoft.Support/supportPlanTypes alias is in preview and may require enabling the Microsoft.Support resource provider on the management group scope.
Practical Decision Guide
Situation
Recommended Action
Personal learning or side project
Basic is sufficient — use docs, Q&A, and Service Health
Non-production dev/test subscription
Basic is acceptable — developer forums cover most questions
Production workload < $1k/mo Azure spend
Developer ($29/mo) adds business-hours tech support
Production with any revenue-impacting SLA
Standard ($100/mo) minimum — 24x7 Sev A response
Regulated workload or enterprise with many subscriptions
Professional Direct or Premier — dedicated advisory and case management
Working Effectively Without Technical Support

Teams on the Basic plan need a structured approach to self-service resolution. The following workflow covers the three most common failure categories — connectivity, performance, and configuration — using free Azure tooling.
Connectivity Troubleshooting with Network Watcher

Network Watcher provides packet capture, connectivity check, and next-hop analysis — all available without a paid support plan. These tools cover the majority of VM-to-VM and VM-to-internet connectivity issues.

# Check connectivity from a VM to a destination (requires Network Watcher extension on source VM)
az network watcher test-connectivity \
  --source-resource "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/my-vm" \
  --dest-address "10.0.2.4" \
  --dest-port 5432 \
  --output table

# Next-hop analysis — useful for diagnosing routing failures
az network watcher show-next-hop \
  --vm "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/my-vm" \
  --nic "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Network/networkInterfaces/my-nic" \
  --source-ip "10.0.1.4" \
  --dest-ip "10.0.2.4" \
  --output table

# IP flow verify — check if NSG rules allow a specific flow
az network watcher test-ip-flow \
  --vm "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/my-vm" \
  --direction Inbound \
  --protocol TCP \
  --local "10.0.1.4:443" \
  --remote "0.0.0.0:*" \
  --output table
App Service Diagnostic Logging
App Service emits application logs, HTTP access logs, and failed-request traces that can be streamed without a support case. Enable them via CLI and tail the live log stream for real-time debugging.

# Enable all diagnostic log categories for an App Service
az webapp log config \
  --name my-api \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --application-logging filesystem \
  --web-server-logging filesystem \
  --detailed-error-messages true \
  --failed-request-tracing true

# Stream live logs to the terminal
az webapp log tail --name my-api --resource-group my-rg

# Download a 48-hour log archive
az webapp log download \
  --name my-api \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --log-file /tmp/app-logs-$(date +%Y%m%d).zip
VM Boot Diagnostics

When a VM fails to start, Boot Diagnostics captures serial console output and a screenshot of the display state at boot. This covers kernel panics, disk failures, and misconfigured network interfaces — all diagnosable without a support case.

import requests, base64
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_boot_diagnostics(subscription_id: str, resource_group: str, vm_name: str) -> dict:
    """Retrieve boot diagnostics log URI and screenshot for a VM."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/resourceGroups/{resource_group}/providers/Microsoft.Compute"
        f"/virtualMachines/{vm_name}/retrieveBootDiagnosticsData"
        f"?api-version=2023-03-01"
    )
    resp = requests.post(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()

    return {
        "console_log_uri": data.get("serialConsoleLogBlobUri"),
        "screenshot_uri": data.get("screenshotBlobUri"),
    }

def download_serial_log(log_uri: str, dest_path: str) -> None:
    """Download the serial console log from the SAS URI."""
    resp = requests.get(log_uri, timeout=60)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    with open(dest_path, "wb") as f:
        f.write(resp.content)
    print(f"Serial log saved to {dest_path} ({len(resp.content)} bytes)")

diag = get_boot_diagnostics("your-sub", "my-rg", "my-vm")
if diag["console_log_uri"]:
    download_serial_log(diag["console_log_uri"], "/tmp/serial.log")
Using Azure Resource Health

Resource Health reports the health state of individual resources in real time. Unlike Service Health (which tracks platform-wide incidents), Resource Health targets your specific instance — a single VM, SQL database, or Key Vault.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_resource_health(resource_id: str) -> dict:
    """Return the current health state and annotations for a specific resource."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{resource_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/availabilityStatuses/current"
        f"?api-version=2022-10-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    props = resp.json().get("properties", {})

    return {
        "availability_state": props.get("availabilityState"),   # Available / Unavailable / Unknown
        "summary": props.get("summary"),
        "reason_type": props.get("reasonType"),
        "occured_time": props.get("occuredTime"),
        "reason_chronicity": props.get("reasonChronicity"),      # Persistent / Transient
    }

def scan_resource_health(resource_ids: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    """Check health state for multiple resources and return unhealthy ones."""
    results = []
    for rid in resource_ids:
        health = get_resource_health(rid)
        if health["availability_state"] != "Available":
            results.append({"resource": rid, **health})
    return results

RESOURCE_IDS = [
    "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/api",
    "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Sql/servers/sql/databases/prod-db",
]
unhealthy = scan_resource_health(RESOURCE_IDS)
for r in unhealthy:
    print(f"[{r['availability_state']}] {r['resource'].split('/')[-1]} — {r['summary']}")
Setting Up Proactive Alerting with Basic Plan

Without a paid support plan, your incident response depends entirely on detecting issues yourself. A minimal alerting stack — Azure Monitor action groups, metric alerts, and Service Health alerts — is free or low-cost and catches most production issues before users escalate.
Service Health Alert Rule

# Create an action group (email + webhook)
az monitor action-group create \
  --name "ops-team" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --short-name "ops" \
  --email-receiver name=oncall email=oncall@company.com \
  --webhook-receiver name=teams service-uri="https://outlook.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/..."

# Create a Service Health alert that fires on any active issue in East US
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name "service-health-eastus" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/your-sub-id" \
  --condition "category=ServiceHealth" \
  --action-group "/subscriptions/your-sub-id/resourceGroups/my-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team" \
  --description "Fire on any Azure service health event"

# Metric alert: App Service HTTP 5xx > 10 in 5 minutes
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name "app-5xx-alert" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/my-api" \
  --condition "count Http5xx > 10" \
  --window-size 5m \
  --evaluation-frequency 1m \
  --action "/subscriptions/your-sub-id/resourceGroups/my-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team" \
  --severity 2 \
  --description "High 5xx rate on API"

# Metric alert: VM CPU > 90% for 15 minutes
az monitor metrics alert create \
  --name "vm-high-cpu" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/{sub}/resourceGroups/{rg}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/my-vm" \
  --condition "avg Percentage CPU > 90" \
  --window-size 15m \
  --evaluation-frequency 5m \
  --action "/subscriptions/your-sub-id/resourceGroups/my-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team" \
  --severity 2
Automated Runbook as First-Responder

Azure Automation Runbooks can act as a first-responder when an alert fires, collecting diagnostics before a human looks at the issue — essential when you cannot open a support case and need to build your own evidence trail.

# Azure Automation Runbook (Python 3.8 runtime)
# Triggered by alert action group webhook; collects metrics and logs
import automationassets
import json
import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def get_credential_token(resource: str = "https://management.azure.com/") -> str:
    conn = automationassets.get_automation_connection("AzureRunAsConnection")
    from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
    cred = ClientSecretCredential(
        tenant_id=conn["TenantId"],
        client_id=conn["ApplicationId"],
        client_secret=conn["CertificateThumbprint"],  # or use certificate auth
    )
    return cred.get_token(resource).token

def collect_vm_snapshot(subscription_id: str, resource_group: str, vm_name: str) -> dict:
    token = get_credential_token()
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    # VM instance view (power state, agent status)
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/resourceGroups/{resource_group}/providers/Microsoft.Compute"
        f"/virtualMachines/{vm_name}/instanceView?api-version=2023-03-01"
    )
    view = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=15).json()

    statuses = {s["code"]: s.get("displayStatus") for s in view.get("statuses", [])}
    return {
        "vm": vm_name,
        "power_state": statuses.get("PowerState"),
        "provisioning_state": statuses.get("ProvisioningState"),
        "collected_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    }

# Main entry point
snapshot = collect_vm_snapshot(
    automationassets.get_automation_variable("SubscriptionId"),
    automationassets.get_automation_variable("ResourceGroup"),
    automationassets.get_automation_variable("VmName"),
)
print(json.dumps(snapshot, indent=2))
Summary

Azure Basic Support is automatically assigned to all subscriptions at no cost and provides billing support, Azure Service Health access, Azure Advisor recommendations, and access to self-help documentation and community forums.
No technical support cases — only billing and subscription support tickets can be opened.
Azure Service Health is free and provides personalised outage alerts without a paid plan.
Azure Advisor analyses your resources and recommends cost, reliability, and security improvements at no charge.
Upgrade to Developer ($29/mo) for business-hours technical support, or Standard ($100/mo) for 24x7 Severity A coverage.
Enterprises managing many subscriptions should audit support plan assignments via policy and subscription vending pipelines.
